name: crocus sativus
mechanism: hypothesized mechanisms include serotonergic modulation and antioxidant/anti-inflammatory signaling | mechanistic certainty remains incomplete
interactions: Potential interactions with serotonergic or CNS-active medicines require medication-level review.
safetyNotes: Short-term controlled use is often tolerated, but concentrated extracts can differ from culinary exposure.
description: Crocus sativus (saffron) is a culinary and medicinal spice with modern randomized-trial syntheses for mood-related outcomes.
dosage: Dose-response depends on standardized extract and indication; no single cross-product dose is appropriate.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. preparation: Used as culinary stigma material and as standardized oral extracts in clinical research.
region: Cultivated historically in Iran and the Mediterranean, now distributed globally.
traditionalUse: Traditional Persian and Mediterranean medical systems include saffron for mood and general vitality support.
